collection.name: EAC11 Heritage Collection
collection.source: EAC Guidelines 11 — Articulating the Significance of Archaeological Sites (2026)

sites.name: Carnuntum
sites.description: Over 10 km² of Roman frontier landscape: legionary fortress, canabae, auxiliary fort, civil town, amphitheatres, sanctuaries, burial grounds. Over 90% unexcavated, preserved underground beneath farmland.
sites.significanceSummary: Largest archaeological cultural landscape in Austria and the only Roman legionary-fortress complex where complete urban surroundings survive without modern overbuilding. UNESCO Danube Limes component.
sites.collectionHighlight: Largest site in collection; only UNESCO World Heritage component
sites.integrityNote: 90%+ preserved underground; geophysical survey confirmed full-area preservation
sites.comparativeBasis: Completeness + non-overbuilt status; all comparable Austrian Roman towns overbuilt
sites.method: Austrian Monument Protection Act + UNESCO OUV criteria

sites.name: Pitschenbergalm
sites.description: High-altitude alpine pasture (1730–1930 m) with 19 documented structures: huts, stables, pens, enclosures, lime kiln. Visible as dry-set stone ruins. Still used for sheep grazing.
sites.significanceSummary: Almost four millennia of seasonal alpine land use in a compact area — an 'archive in the ground' for alpine pastoralism. Among the highest-ranking archaeological monuments in Austria.
sites.collectionHighlight: Highest altitude site; longest continuous use span (~4,000 years)
sites.integrityNote: Particularly high intactness and authentic preservation; remote location prevented disturbance
sites.comparativeBasis: Multi-period diversity + research depth + spatial compactness among Austrian alpine sites
sites.method: Austrian law: quality, quantity, diversity, dispersion

sites.name: Kurese
sites.description: Abandoned village with 26 monuments: stone graves, clearance cairns, cup-marked stones, ramparts, field banks, settlement layers. Stone fences preserved. Connected to Soontagana stronghold. Buffer zone ~900 ha.
sites.significanceSummary: Uniquely preserved agricultural landscape where entire village structure survived intact — Soviet deportation emptied it and the land was unsuitable for collective agriculture. 'Invaluable resource for study of Bronze Age and Iron Age society.'
sites.collectionHighlight: Preservation caused by historical tragedy (Soviet deportation)
sites.integrityNote: Exceptional — no modern disturbance; Soviet abandonment + unsuitable for agriculture
sites.comparativeBasis: Completeness of preserved landscape structure; exceptional buffer zone in Estonian practice
sites.protectionNote: 26 monuments registered (2018) with common buffer zone
sites.method: NHB criteria: age, location, scientific value, uniqueness, prominence

sites.name: Raseborg Castle
sites.description: Castle ruin on a rocky hill (originally island). Ring wall, towers, courtyard, two outer castles, round tower, underwater piling. Adjacent Bastuåkern: medieval trading area. Abandoned ~1550.
sites.significanceSummary: Only Finnish medieval stone castle where both the castle and its outer yard are exceptionally preserved — abandoned in the 16th century, never resettled, never cultivated. 16/17 points in national significance assessment.
sites.collectionHighlight: Highest score in collection (16/17); only site with quantified illegal detecting damage
sites.integrityNote: Well preserved; ~300 pits by illegal detectorists (2017), most structures untouched
sites.comparativeBasis: Only one of 8 Finnish medieval castles with preserved surroundings

sites.name: Vrouw Maria
sites.description: Dutch merchant vessel, ~26 m long. ~90% hull preserved at 41 m depth. Cargo: art/silver for Catherine the Great, trade goods. Discovered 1999. In Archipelago National Park.
sites.significanceSummary: Exceptionally preserved 18th-century merchant ship — concrete manifestation of Baltic trade between Western Europe and Russia. Catherine the Great art cargo. Only one comparable vessel known worldwide.
sites.collectionHighlight: Only underwater site; most internationally connected (Russia–Netherlands trade)
sites.integrityNote: ~90% hull intact at 41 m; cold Baltic preservation; most cargo in hold
sites.comparativeBasis: Only one comparable snow-rigged ship (Sjöhästen, Sweden — warship). Hull completeness + cargo + archival documentation
sites.protectionNote: Antiquities Act + 1,500 m restriction zone + National Park

sites.name: Tumuli Hungary
sites.description: Thousands of tumuli across Hungary. Bárdos-halom: 2.7 m high, 75×55 m, under cultivation. Multi-period surroundings. 74 kurgans in Füzesgyarmat area, 20 with landscape importance.
sites.significanceSummary: Tumuli are complex phenomena: landmarks along ancient routes, sites of folk traditions, often topped by medieval churches. Significance extends into landscape identity and nature conservation (ancient steppe vegetation). Most lack formal protection.
sites.collectionHighlight: Only site explicitly addressing nature conservation value; largest number of individual features
sites.integrityNote: Highly variable; many ploughed flat; Bárdos-halom well preserved despite ploughing
sites.comparativeBasis: Internal ranking by 4 categories (landscape prominence + vegetation + historical associations)
sites.protectionNote: Most not protected; Nature Act covers 1,548 tumuli; few have archaeological protection

sites.name: Pilis Mountains
sites.fullName: Pilis Mountains and Szentendre Island Programme
sites.description: Region with prehistoric hilltops, Roman Limes fortifications, medieval royal seats, monasteries. 21 sites surveyed by CEU + Ferenczy Museum + Community Archaeology Association.
sites.significanceSummary: Methodological demonstration that significance assessment can integrate academic criteria with social values and community participation. Most detailed scoring system in this collection (5 groups, 0–3, total 71 points).
sites.collectionHighlight: Most comprehensive assessment methodology; only community archaeology programme
sites.integrityNote: Variable; scored per site (0–3 for preservation and threat exposure)
sites.comparativeBasis: Internal scoring enables ranking within region
sites.protectionNote: 11 of 21 sites specially protected; rest registered
sites.method: Spreadsheet: 5 groups, multiple sub-criteria, 0–3 scoring. Total 71 pts

sites.name: Hveravellir
sites.description: Geothermal area (~2.5 km²) on old Kjölur mountain route. 19 remains: outlaw huts (Mountain-Eyvind), transport cairns, traveler shelter houses. Hot springs, lava fields.
sites.significanceSummary: Nature and culture inseparable: geothermal features enabled survival in uninhabitable highlands. Outlaw remains scored 9/10 — unique nationally, rare, authentic, experientially powerful. 'Every child in Iceland knows' the Mountain-Eyvind stories.
sites.collectionHighlight: Strongest intangible heritage connection; nature-culture integration
sites.integrityNote: Ruins untampered, 'except by the unkind forces of nature'; remote highland protection
sites.comparativeBasis: Unique nationally for outlaw remains; nothing comparable. Saga connections + experiential value
sites.protectionNote: 100-year automatic protection; not on listed sites register
sites.method: 10 value ranges (adapted SAVE), +/0 scoring. Outlaw remains: 9/10

sites.name: Þjórsárdalur
sites.description: Fork-shaped valley with ~20 farmsteads. 122 m Háifoss waterfall. Excavated Stöng (famous Viking longhouse). First systematic archaeological survey in Iceland (mid-19th c.). 140 km² protected area.
sites.significanceSummary: Settlement landscape 'frozen' in the early 12th century by Hekla eruption — never reinhabited. Birthplace of Icelandic archaeology (1939 excavations established tephrochronology). First whole-valley protection in Iceland (140 km²).
sites.collectionHighlight: Largest protected area (140 km²); volcanic 'time capsule'
sites.integrityNote: Exceptional — frozen in 12th century; volcanic ash sealed remains; untouched by later use
sites.comparativeBasis: First whole-valley cultural landscape protection in Iceland; completeness + volcanic sealing
sites.method: Cultural landscape protection process; no formal scoring

sites.name: Waterford
sites.description: Development site straddling 13th-century town wall within Viking-founded city. Upstanding medieval wall, Dominican priory remains, pre-wall bank, ditch, tower base. Wall displayed in retail premises.
sites.significanceSummary: Significance assessment directly determined preservation outcomes: discovery of town wall triggered national policy (town defences = single national monument). Demonstrates interplay of law, policy, and professional assessment in urban heritage.
sites.collectionHighlight: Clearest case of policy-driven preservation; heritage integrated into commercial space
sites.integrityNote: Town wall intact in later fabric; subsurface variable; some excavated, some in situ
sites.comparativeBasis: Town defences treated as single national monument (2008 policy)
sites.protectionNote: National Monuments Acts; town wall = national monument
sites.method: Irish planning process: referral → assessment → consent → conditions

sites.name: Tuba-Zangariyye
sites.description: 65+ dolmens and 20+ tumuli over ~0.5 km² on basalt hill. Part of Korazim field (~500 dolmens). Some covering stones up to 15 tons. First documented 1877 by Kitchener.
sites.significanceSummary: Largest dolmen field west of Jordan and one of the densest megalithic burial fields in the world. After destruction elsewhere, this is 'a rare and almost the last remnant of this cultural landscape.' Assessment reversed a destructive development plan. Only CBSA case.
sites.collectionHighlight: Only site using CBSA; assessment reversed destruction; oldest remains in collection
sites.integrityNote: Field well preserved; wider Korazim Plateau severely impacted — dozens destroyed
sites.comparativeBasis: Largest west of Jordan; one of densest globally. Density + preservation in diminished landscape
sites.protectionNote: Registered but no formal preservation tools; development plan conditionally approved

sites.name: Leithon
sites.description: Settlement on Rhine estuary channels. Revetments of timber, wickerwork, re-used Roman material. ~200,000 finds from 2013 excavation. Part of Frisian Trade network. 40 cm below surface.
sites.significanceSummary: One of the last well-preserved early medieval settlements in heavily urbanized western Netherlands. Part of Frisian Trade network. Compromise: partial excavation (~200,000 finds) + partial in-situ scheduling. 'Archive for future generations.'
sites.collectionHighlight: Most finds recovered (200,000); shallowest remains (40 cm); preservation compromise model
sites.integrityNote: Excellent organic preservation; settlement ended abruptly (838 storm surge); partial excavation
sites.comparativeBasis: Last surviving example in urbanized region; compared to Dorestad. Preservation + rarity in context
sites.method: Valletta Convention principle; CHA + local negotiation

sites.name: Zamczysko
sites.description: Egg-shaped ringfort on wooded dune edge. Double ramparts, two moats. ~102×78 m; inner rampart ~5 m high. Old-growth forest. In Kampinos National Park strict protection. WWII partisan base.
sites.significanceSummary: A 'time capsule' — original strata preserved in situ within national park strict protection. Both archaeology and cultural landscape protected for centuries. Model of nature-heritage collaboration. WWII symbolic value.
sites.collectionHighlight: Best nature-heritage integration; WWII dimension unique in collection
sites.integrityNote: Original strata in situ; protected by forest and strict protection for centuries
sites.comparativeBasis: Unique combination: archaeological preservation + nature strict protection + capital proximity
sites.protectionNote: Registered monument (1965) + National Park strict protection + UNESCO Biosphere

sites.name: Gdańsk
sites.fullName: Church of St Nicholas and Romanesque Cellar, Gdańsk
sites.description: Romanesque church foundations (34.5 m, ~1190) beneath 1896 Market Hall. Nearby: Romanesque cellar — only surviving brick late-Romanesque building in Gdańsk — vaulted refectory, 60% preserved. Both now public.
sites.significanceSummary: Oldest monumental architecture in Gdańsk — pre-Teutonic history discovered during commercial renovation. Community and professional protest reversed the decision to destroy the cellar. Shows urban heritage can coexist with commercial space.
sites.collectionHighlight: Only site saved by community protest; heritage discovery during commercial works
sites.integrityNote: Church foundations preserved; cellar 60% with vault; conservation + partial reconstruction
sites.comparativeBasis: Only surviving brick late-Romanesque building in Gdańsk; local uniqueness + Dominican connection
sites.protectionNote: Market Hall registered monument; cellar museum since 2015
sites.method: Criteria in letter to GMPO: uniqueness, community, historical/symbolic, preservation

sites.name: Dumbarton Rock
sites.description: Volcanic plug, 73 m, on Firth of Clyde. Twin peaks with remains from Iron Age through WWII. Capital of Kingdom of Strathclyde. Multiple designations: SAM, Listed Buildings, SSSI. Known to climbers as 'Dumby.'
sites.significanceSummary: Where formal heritage and living community heritage converge. First community-contributed Statement of Significance in Scotland (rock climbers). Climbers formed 'close physical and psychological connections with the materiality of the rock' — place-based intangible heritage.
sites.collectionHighlight: Only living/active heritage; first community co-produced significance statement
sites.integrityNote: Rock permanent; archaeological variable; climbing heritage ongoing and living
sites.comparativeBasis: Comparable to Edinburgh/Stirling castle rocks; unique in community heritage dimension
sites.protectionNote: SAM + Listed Buildings + SSSI; HES site in care
sites.method: HES Statement of Significance + ACCORD community co-production
